{
  "analysis": "The training data reveals a consistent pattern for German legal documents: Person entities appear in two main contexts. 1) As the subject of a complaint ('Beschwerdesache [Name]') or 'Beschwerde der [Name]'. 2) As the judge/official ('durch den Richter [Name]' or 'durch die Richterin [Name]'). The names often include academic titles (Mag., Dr., Prof., Dipl.-Ing., etc.) and suffixes (LL.M., BA, BSc, etc.). The challenge is to capture the full name including titles without capturing the surrounding legal context (like 'Richter' or 'Beschwerdesache'). The patterns must handle variable title lengths and suffixes. The 'Sibylle Mehlbeer' examples show that names in quotes are also valid entities. The 'Erich Hlavaty' example shows that gender markers ('Frau') can precede names, but the name itself is the entity.",
  "strategy": "I will create a set of focused regex rules. Rule 1 targets names following 'Richter' or 'Richterin' (including titles). Rule 2 targets names following 'Beschwerdesache' or 'Beschwerde der'. Rule 3 targets names in quotes. Rule 4 handles names preceded by 'Herr' or 'Frau'. All rules will use capture groups to isolate the name part, ensuring precision. I will prioritize the specific context rules over generic name patterns to avoid false positives.",

    {
      "name": "Judge_Richter",
      "description": "Captures person names following 'Richter' or 'Richterin' (including titles like Mag., Dr., Prof.).",

      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "person"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 10,
      "reasoning": "High priority rule for judges. Captures the full name including titles (Mag., Dr., Prof.) and suffixes (LL.M.) that follow the judge title. Uses context to ensure it's a judge."
    },
    {
      "name": "Complainant_Beschwerdesache",
      "description": "Captures person names following 'Beschwerdesache' or 'Beschwerde der'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "(?:in\s+der\s+Beschwerdesache\s+|Beschwerde\s+der\s+|Beschwerde\s+des\s+)([A-Z][a-zäöüß]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-zäöüß]+)*(?:\s+\.?[A-Z]{1,3}(?:\.?[A-Z]{1,3})*)*)",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "person"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 9,
      "reasoning": "Captures the complainant's name which is a very common entity type in these documents. Handles both 'in der Beschwerdesache' and 'Beschwerde der/des' structures."
    },

This handles cases like "Sibylle Mehlbeer" where the name is used as a label for a non-existent entity or group name that is actually a person's name in context. We capture the name inside the quotes if it looks like a person's name (Capitalized words). However, to be safe and precise, we only capture if it's clearly a name pattern inside quotes, not just any quoted text. WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.

  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Return JSON:

  "note": "Every rule must include both output_template and output_key",

      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 1-10 (higher = more important),
      "reasoning": "Why this rule is needed"
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": ["name_of_rule_to_remove (optional, only when replacing a bad rule)"]
}

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 20.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

metadata.analysis: The system suffers from severe over-extraction due to overly generic patterns (e.g., matching titles like 'Mag.Dr' or legal context words like 'Beschwerdesache' as names) and a lack of negative lookahead constraints to prevent capturing non-person entities. The priority system is ineffective because high-priority rules often capture partial or incorrect spans that lower-priority rules cannot fix, leading to fragmented entities and false positives.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 8.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

USER GUIDANCE (task-level feedback):
- 1. **Consolidate and Refine Patterns**: The current ruleset is fragmented. Many rules match the same context (e.g., 'Beschwerdesache') but with different levels of precision. Consolidate these into a few robust rules that use negative lookaheads to exclude non-person entities (e.g., 'Beschwerdesache', 'Rechtssache', 'Verfahren', 'Gruppe', 'GmbH').
2. **Priority Reordering**: Currently, high-priority rules often capture partial or incorrect spans. Reorder priorities so that rules with strict negative lookaheads (high precision) run first, followed by rules that capture suffixes or full names. Ensure that rules capturing the *full* name (including suffixes) have higher priority than rules capturing just the name.
3. **Add Negative Lookaheads**: The most critical missing element is negative lookahead logic to exclude legal context words, company names, and titles that are not followed by a name. Add patterns like `(?! Beschwerdesache | Rechtssache | Gruppe | GmbH )` to all name-matching rules.
4. **Handle Suffixes Explicitly**: Many rules fail to capture suffixes (e.g., 'MA Bakk. art.'). Create a dedicated rule or extend existing rules to explicitly capture the optional suffix pattern after the name.
5. **Remove Redundant Rules**: Many rules (e.g., `Name_After_Comma_Address`, `Name_After_Comma_Suffix`) are identical or nearly identical. Merge them to reduce complexity and potential conflicts.
6. **Human-in-the-Loop Validation**: For the 'PreNominal_Title_Name' rule, which is the most problematic, consider a two-step approach: first identify the title, then validate that a valid name follows before capturing the entity.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

You are the Refinement Coordinator for a rule learning system.
After each refinement iteration, you analyze per-class performance and guide the next patch.

ITERATION: 3/6
OVERALL: accuracy=18.9%, micro_F1=44.0%, macro_F1=44.0%

PER-CLASS PERFORMANCE (sorted worst to best):

Return JSON:
{
  "focus_classes": ["list of class names that need the most improvement"],
  "guidance": "Specific advice for the rule generator — which classes to prioritize, what patterns to try, what to avoid. 2-3 sentences max.",
  "should_continue": boolean (false if performance is good enough or unlikely to improve further)
}

You are updating an existing rule-based extractor. Do NOT rewrite good rules; add or adjust only what is needed.

Task: German Legal Named Entity Recognition
Description: Recognize named entities in German legal text. Entities to look for: person.

Input schema: {'text': 'str'}
Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label
        
AVAILABLE ENTITY TYPES: ['person']
Rules MUST use one of these types in output_template.


DATA EVIDENCE FROM TRAINING:
- person (15 unique): "Rudolf Hummelt", "Hon.-Prof. Lothar D\u00f6ppkens", "Claire Strotmeyer", "Dr. Irvin Math\u00e4ss", "Zlatan Grensenbach, MA Bakk. art.", "Paul Br\u00e4udigam", "RgR PhD Franka Kopecky", "Mag.a Priv.-Doz.in Hon.-Prof.in Melanie Kreikemeier", "PhD Sascha Bl\u00fcmke", "Justin Feuerheerdt", "Mag. Mag. Hon.-Prof. Gottfried Rabensteiner", "KommR KzlR Finn H\u00e4rtich", "Urs Schelzel", "RgR Peter Schlossnagel", "Matth\u00e4us Marsteller"
  Exact pattern: (?:Ma(?:g\.(?:a Priv\.\-Doz\.in Hon\.\-Prof\.in Melanie Kreikemei| Mag\. Hon\.\-Prof\. Gottfried Rabenstein)er|tthäus Marsteller)|Zlatan Grensenbach, MA Bakk\. art\.|Hon\.\-Prof\. Lothar Döppkens|KommR KzlR Finn Härtich|RgR P(?:eter Schlossnagel|hD Franka Kopecky)|Justin Feuerheerdt|P(?:hD Sascha Blümke|aul Bräudigam)|Claire Strotmeyer|Dr\. Irvin Mathäss|Rudolf Hummelt|Urs Schelzel)
  In context: "... Beschwerdesache [Rudolf Hummelt], Zuberbach-Neuhof...", "...durch den Richter [Hon.-Prof. Lothar D\u00f6ppkens]  in der...", "...Beschwerdesache [Claire Strotmeyer],  Am Liding 12,...", "...durch den Richter [Dr. Irvin Math\u00e4ss]  in der...", "...Beschwerdesache [Zlatan Grensenbach, MA Bakk. art.], ..."

Note: Some entities contain multiple words; do NOT assume single-token matches.

Computed patterns match training strings only; generalize carefully.

USER GUIDANCE (task-level feedback):
- 1. **Consolidate and Refine Patterns**: The current ruleset is fragmented. Many rules match the same context (e.g., 'Beschwerdesache') but with different levels of precision. Consolidate these into a few robust rules that use negative lookaheads to exclude non-person entities (e.g., 'Beschwerdesache', 'Rechtssache', 'Verfahren', 'Gruppe', 'GmbH').
2. **Priority Reordering**: Currently, high-priority rules often capture partial or incorrect spans. Reorder priorities so that rules with strict negative lookaheads (high precision) run first, followed by rules that capture suffixes or full names. Ensure that rules capturing the *full* name (including suffixes) have higher priority than rules capturing just the name.
3. **Add Negative Lookaheads**: The most critical missing element is negative lookahead logic to exclude legal context words, company names, and titles that are not followed by a name. Add patterns like `(?! Beschwerdesache | Rechtssache | Gruppe | GmbH )` to all name-matching rules.
4. **Handle Suffixes Explicitly**: Many rules fail to capture suffixes (e.g., 'MA Bakk. art.'). Create a dedicated rule or extend existing rules to explicitly capture the optional suffix pattern after the name.
5. **Remove Redundant Rules**: Many rules (e.g., `Name_After_Comma_Address`, `Name_After_Comma_Suffix`) are identical or nearly identical. Merge them to reduce complexity and potential conflicts.
6. **Human-in-the-Loop Validation**: For the 'PreNominal_Title_Name' rule, which is the most problematic, consider a two-step approach: first identify the title, then validate that a valid name follows before capturing the entity.


COORDINATOR GUIDANCE (prioritize this):
Prioritize reducing the high false positive rate (869 FP) for the 'person' class by refining exclusion rules for background objects or textures that mimic human features. Avoid overly broad positive triggers and instead focus on adding specific spatial or structural constraints to improve precision without sacrificing the current recall.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.

For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Entity:

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
